coredata.dc:title: Magneto-fluorescent core–shell Sr0.8La0.2Fe11CuO19 @ CQDs for the detection of metal ions

coredata.dc:creator: Ateia, Ebtesam E.
Rabie, Obaida
Mohamed, Amira T.
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of a sensor based on a core–shell structure of Sr0.8La0.2Fe11CuO19 @ CQDs was effectively achieved through the combination of high quantum yield carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with La-Cu doped M−type strontium hexaferrites (SLFCO HF). The structural, morphological, and spectroscopic information of the as-synthesized samples were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy (RS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy. The magnetic properties were tested using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and Mössbauer spectroscopy. HR-TEM confirmed the formation of a hexagonal core–shell sample at the nanoscale. Based on photoluminescence (PL) spectra and the CIE chromaticity map, the parent sample and core–shell structure of SLFCO/CQDs were found to have color coordinates corresponding to yellow-green emission. It was found that the core–shell sample has an increased light-absorption capacity, low photogenerated electron-hole recombination, high coercivity, small crystallite size, and showed good sensitivity towards the detection of Zn2+, Cd2+, and K+ ions. A high efficiency in detecting potassium (K+) ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 15 ppm was observed. Moreover, the distinctive magnetism of CQDs @ SLFCO aided in the collection and recycling of the sensor.

head.ce:title: Magneto-fluorescent core–shell Sr0.8La0.2Fe11CuO19 @ CQDs for the detection of metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Cairo University, Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Cairo University, Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of a sensor based on a core–shell structure of Sr0.8La0.2Fe11CuO19 @ CQDs was effectively achieved through the combination of high quantum yield carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with La-Cu doped M−type strontium hexaferrites (SLFCO HF). The structural, morphological, and spectroscopic information of the as-synthesized samples were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy (RS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy. The magnetic properties were tested using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and Mössbauer spectroscopy. HR-TEM confirmed the formation of a hexagonal core–shell sample at the nanoscale. Based on photoluminescence (PL) spectra and the CIE chromaticity map, the parent sample and core–shell structure of SLFCO/CQDs were found to have color coordinates corresponding to yellow-green emission. It was found that the core–shell sample has an increased light-absorption capacity, low photogenerated electron-hole recombination, high coercivity, small crystallite size, and showed good sensitivity towards the detection of Zn2+, Cd2+, and K+ ions. A high efficiency in detecting potassium (K+) ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 15 ppm was observed. Moreover, the distinctive magnetism of CQDs @ SLFCO aided in the collection and recycling of the sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All the data supporting the findings of this study are included within the article

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, environmental protection, particularly water pollution treatment, has garnered significant interest due to the rising levels of environmental contamination [1,2].

Green nanotechnology is one of the most anticipated environmental protection solutions and is receiving significant interest from academia and industry in the field of water pollution removal [3,4]. Among several nanomaterials, green synthesized CQDs have stood out owing to their small nanosized range, high stability, great water solubility, extremely high quantum yield, and tunable fluorescence characteristics [5]. Because a single nanomaterial has limited characteristics, scientists discovered that heterogeneous composite or sandwich colloidal systems of different nanomaterials are more efficient than their comparable single material and, in some situations, even acquire new characteristics [6,7]. Consequently, the phrase “core/shell” was later used.

Core/shell nanoparticles (CSNPs) are steadily garnering more and more attention, due to their emerging status at the intersection of materials chemistry and several other domains, including electronics, biology, pharmaceuticals, optics, and catalysis. Significant differences in properties can originate from changing the core-to-shell ratio or the materials that make up the structure [8]. Karele et al.'s review paper provides an overview of the many applications of CSNPs that are currently in use [9]. Therefore, it is a difficult task to classify all existing CSNPs based on some other attribute or their industrial uses. The four major types of CSNPs distinguished by their material properties are inorganic/inorganic, inorganic/organic, organic/inorganic, and organic/organic core–shell structures [10]. A magnetic material core surrounded by a polymer shell, or any other high-density organic material can be found in inorganic/organic CSNPs. Magnetic material alone cannot fulfill the requirements of a wide range of applications. The oxidation stability of the metal core in a normal environment is one of the main concerns. Additionally, the high tendency of the particles to aggregate and rapid biodegradation in biological systems are major disadvantages of using magnetic NPs for a certain area. As a result, coating magnetic NPs with an organic shell has gained importance. Magnetic NPs with any organic coating are mainly used for magnetic recording, magnetic sealing, electromagnetic shielding, and especially in the biological field and metal ion sensing applications [11,12]. CQDs shell is recognized for their excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, and eco-friendliness. These characteristics make them ideal for use in biomedical applications involving QDs. It can also provide effective passivation of the QDs surface, thereby decreasing non-radiative recombination and enhancing the PL quantum yield.

In contrast, inorganic shells, while they can offer excellent electronic passivation and enhance the optical properties, may suffer from issues such as lattice mismatch with the ferrite core, potentially leading to strain and defects. Furthermore, certain inorganic materials may pose toxicity issues or be less appropriate for specific biological applications [10].

Strontium hexaferrite (SHF) is considered one of the magnetic materials with unique physical properties. The ongoing goal of substitution or doping is to create new materials with improved qualities. Previous studies have reported on the impact of lanthanum-copper (La-Cu) substitution on the alteration of the physical characteristics of SHF [13,14]. Core-shell of magnetic nano-ferrite core and CQDs as a shell present a challenging system. Several studies have previously explored the use of ferrite/CQDs NPs in photodegradation, tumor imaging, and microwave absorption [15–18].

Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, the synergistic impact of La-Cu substitution on SHF and the creation of their core–shell structure with CQDs has not been reported until now. This could be an effective strategy for enhancing the potential applications of the SHF-CQD core–shell system.

The light emitted by a molecule in its excited state (fluorescence or phosphorescence) can be quenched by interacting with other molecules. Thus, for the detection of metal ions, great progress has been made by using all kinds of QDs. For example, CdTe QDs were utilized for the detection of Zn2+
[19], ZnS QDs for Cr3+
[20], and CdTe QDs for Pb2+ and Co2+ [21]. For CQDs, several works on the multiple metal ions detection such as Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ag1+, K+, and Cd2+
[22–25], were reported. Nonetheless, few reports were conducted on the selectivity against Pb2+, Co2+, and As3+ [26–29]. Potassium ions (K+) for example, play key roles in biological systems [30]. Abnormal K+ concentration is a symptom of several diseases [31]. Thus, in order to aid in diagnosis and therapy, selective and sensitive K+ sensors are crucial for detecting physiological potassium levels.

In the present work, fluorescence spectra will be recorded for various metal ions and the fluorescence intensity will be measured after quencher molecules (K+) have been added at various concentrations.

The combination of the magnetic characteristics of SLFCO HF and the fluorescent properties of CQDs in one compound introduces a new bimodal “two-in-one” magnetic-fluorescent core–shell nanomaterial which is the main challenge. Our study focuses on utilizing green synthesized CQDs to encapsulate the La-Cu doped M−type Sr-hexaferrite matrix through an eco-friendly approach. A crucial aspect of our research is the use of the synthesized inorganic/organic CSNPs as a metal ion sensor.

The preparation technique is segmented into three sections. In the first section, the modified citrate auto-combustion technique was used to prepare SLFCO HFs. In the second section, a green process was used to create CQDs nanoparticles (NPs). The third section involved employing the sonication approach to encapsulate the prepared HFs matrix with the green synthesized CQDs NPs.

To match the stoichiometric ratios of the nitrates, the precursors were added in appropriate proportions. Additional details regarding the preparation plan are available in our earlier research [14] and presented in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from orange juice (OR-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A green hydrothermal approach is used to obtain CQDs from orange juice (OR-CQDs) as follows. A Teflon autoclave containing 50 ml of freshly squeezed orange juice was heated to 180 °C for eight hours. After reaching ambient temperature, the sample was filtered to obtain fluorescent OR-CQDs as illustrated in Fig. 1.

0.8 g of SLFCO were added to 20 ml of distilled water. Additionally, 20 ml of OR-CQDs were added to another set of beakers, and the two solutions were sonicated separately for 4 h. After that, the OR-CQDs were added to the SLFCO solution. Then, the mixtures were sonicated for another 4 h, as described in Fig. 1.

Typically, 20 ppm of various metal salts were prepared, and then 10 mg of SLFCO/CQDs core–shell NPs were added to the aqueous solution at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 285 nm without any pretreatment.

The samples’ structure was initially investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with the Diano corporation (Cu-
K
α 1
= 1.5418 \AA). The morphology of the samples was examined by HR-TEM with the Thermo Scientific Talos F200i (S)TEM. The room temperature Raman spectra (RS) were obtained in the range of 100–4000 cm−1 using Horiba LabRam HR Evolution, Horiba, Ltd., UK. Additionally, the room temperature FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet FTIR spectrophotometer (Impact-410, Madison, USA). The samples' magnetic characteristics at room temperature were studied by the vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) model, Lake Shore 7410, while the room temperature Mössbauer spectroscopy was measured using a 57Fe transmission Mössbauer spectrometer in constant acceleration mode. The Mössbauer source was 57Co diffused in a Rh matrix and the spectrometer was calibrated using
α
-Fe. Finally, the fluorescence of the samples was analyzed by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The PL quantum yield was calculated using the relative method. Rhodamine B (RhB) dissolved in Ethanol (ETOH) was used as the reference substance to determine the QY of the CQDs according to the following equation:

The subscripts r and s refer to the reference and sample, respectively, while m represents the slope of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curve, and n denotes the refractive index (1.33 for distilled water and 1.36 for ETOH).

Non-destructive XRD was performed to identify the crystal structure and confirm the phase purity of the samples, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a). One can index every diffraction peak as the hexagonal phase of SrFe12O19 (ICCD card no. 00–033-1340). The characteristic diffraction peaks seen at 2θ = 30.32°, 32.47°, 34.25°, 37.27°, 40.45°, 42.74°, 55.36°,56.84°, and 66.33° correspond to (110), (107), (114), (203), (205), (206), (217), (2011), and (220) crystal planes of SLFCO magneto plumbite HFs, respectively [14]. The OR-CQDs sample exhibits a broad peak centered at 22.8
°
signifying the amorphous nature. Moreover, the XRD pattern of the core–shell verifies its crystalline structure. The addition of OR-CQDs to the magnetic SLFCO NPs is indicated by a large diffraction peak in the SLFCO/CQDs at 2θ = 20
°
– 24
°
[16].

The peak broadening confirms the sample’s nano-size range and the increase in distortion in the sample [32]. Additionally, the broader the peak, the smaller the crystallite size (D). Therefore, we can predict that the D of the SLFCO/CQDs core–shell is smaller than that of SLFCO NPs.

Upon comparing the obtained results, it is noted that the lattice parameter ‘a’ is almost the same, while a shrinkage in the lattice parameter ‘c’ is detected. This shrinkage in the c also affects the unit cell volume which decreased by about 3

The M−type HF architecture is specified using the (c/a) ratio. If this ratio is less than 3.98, this structure should be considered. In this study, the core and core–shell samples reveal a ratio of less than 3.98 as mentioned in Table 1 .

The crystallite size (D) of the core (SLFCO) and core–shell (CQDs /SLFCO) samples was calculated using the Debye Scherrer equation as follows [34].

One of the ways used to estimate D and ε is to use the Williamson Hall model (W-H). Unfortunately, the obtained W–H plot for the SLFCO/CQDs sample has strong scattering points (not present here), which suggests the possibility of inaccurate results. Therefore, two other different models were used to estimate the D and
ε
of the prepared samples. These models are the size-strain plot (SSP) and the Halder-Wagner approach (HWA) [36], as seen in Fig. 2(b-c) and (d-e), respectively.

The SSP approach assumes that the XRD peak profile is made up of a mixture of Gaussian and Lorentzian functions. The Lorentzian function is associated with size widening, and the Gaussian function is connected to strain broadening. The equation employed in the SSP method is:

The slope of the straight- line gives the value of D, while ε is computed from the intercept of the graphs, and tabulated in Table 1.

On the other side, Peak broadening, according to the HWA, is a symmetric Voigt function resulting from the convolution of the Lorentzian and Gaussian functions [37]. When diffraction peak overlap is small, this approach focuses on the peaks at small and moderate angles.

In the HWA the D value is estimated from the reciprocal of the slope while the intersection is proportional to ε. The obtained values are listed in Table 1.

Compared to the results of the HWA and SSP approaches, the D computed using the Debye-Scherrer equation is higher. While the Debye Scherrer technique simply considers the most intense peak for calculating D, the HWA and SSP approaches take into account the broadening of all diffraction peaks. This is the reason for the discrepancy in the D value determined using the different methods [37].

When studying ceramic materials, it is critical to gain an in-depth understanding of dislocation density (δ) in order to clarify the distribution of voids inside tightly packed crystallites [38]. This essential idea is highly beneficial for the investigation of coating effects. However, the interchange distance (r), and the distortion parameter (g) were estimated as mentioned in the previous work [39]. Variations in “g” and δ directly result from site vacancies and defects in the structure. From Table 1, it is observed that the D decreases but “g”, “δ” and “ε” increase. This alteration in the parameters after coating may result from changing the degree of favored orientation of crystallites with the increase in ε and δ. Hence the degree of preferred orientation may provide crucial characterization of intrinsic defect states in the synthesized material.

More importantly, the characteristic bands of the core–shell sample (
ν 2
a n d
ν 1
) were blue-shifted. This shift can be attributed to the fact that CQDs coated the surface of SLFCO forming a heterogeneous interface that influences the vibrational bands [46,47].

Fig. 3(b) shows the Raman spectra (RS) for OR-CQDs, SLFCO, and SLFCO/CQDs recorded at room temperature (RT) in a broad Raman shift ranging from 100 to 4000 cm−1. 42 Raman active modes are obtained from group theory analysis of HFs based on D6h symmetry. These modes are presentedas irreducible representations 11 A1g + 17 E2g + 14 E1g [48]. There are only 13 active vibration modes on the deconvoluted RS which characterize M−type HFs [14]. They are found at 171, 194, 292, 318, 390, 342, 418, 474, 514, 542, 619, 689, and 724 cm−1 for SLFCO and at 217, 254, 278, 300, 342, 394, 423, 463, 537, 561, 623, 683, and 709 cm−1 for SLFCO/CQDs. These active modes are summarized in Table 2 .

On the other hand, the RT-RS for SLFCO/CQDs reveals two distinct peaks at 1350 and 1555 cm−1 corresponding to the sp3 (D) and sp2 (G) bands of CQDs, respectively [49,50] confirming the presence of OR-CQDs in the prepared core–shell sample. CQDs'graphiticstructureissuggestedbythedominantstrengthofthe Gpeakin the present study.

The typical morphologies of SLFCO/CQDs, OR-CQDs, and SLFCO were investigated using HRTEM. The SLFCO exhibits a dense structure. Most of the particles were aggregated and had a wide particle size distribution, which can be explained by both the particles' magnetic nature and van der Waals forces. (Fig. 4 (a)). Hence, the exact determination of particle sizes is difficult in this case.

Fig. 4(b) shows a uniform and monodispersed morphology of OR-CQDs with an average particle size of 6.2 nm. The OR-CQDs are evenly anchored on the surface of the hexagonal-shaped SLFCO (Fig. 4(c)). In addition, the image's contrast between the NPs shows that the core/shell nanoarchitecture has a brighter fluorescence shell and a darker magnetic core (Fig. 4(d, e)). Noticeably, the SLFCO/CQDs core–shell structure exhibited a significant improvement in the dispersion of these NPs, suggesting that the presence of OR-CQDs may efficiently prevent the SLFCO NPs from aggregating. Fig. 4(f) displays the HRTEM images of SLFCO/CQDs, showing a clear lattice d-spacing of 0.387 nm that can be well indexed to the (002) plane of OR-CQDs, consistent with the XRD spectra. Additionally, the diffraction rings of SLFCO/CQDs are compatible with the hexagonal crystal plane (110, 114, 206, 1011, 300, and 2014) as shown in Fig. 4(g). These results confirm the formation of an inorganic/organic hybrid system of SLFCO coated by OR-CQDs. The average particle distribution curve for the SLFCO/CQDs sample is obtained using ImageJ software and presented in Fig. 4(h) revealing a shell thickness of 7.3 nm and a hexagonal core size of 34.7 nm.
Ultimately, the dispersion of OR-CQDs in the nanohybrid modified the particle size distribution leading to a boosted surface area. This is in line with the results obtained by S. Rashid et al [46].

As seen in Fig. 5 (a-b), the EDX spectra for SLFCO indicate the presence of Sr, La, Fe, Cu, and O, whereas the SLFCO/CQDs spectra reveal the presence of all the previous elements, in addition to C. The EDX spectra are accompanied bythe obtained weight percentage (wt %) and atomic percentage (at %) of the component elements.

The Mössbauer spectrum (MS) has high energy distinguishability, which allows for sensitive testing of the smallest energy variations occurring around the atomic nucleus, making it an effective method for studying the structure of nano-materials. The correlation between the Sr-hexaferrite's structure and magnetic characteristics can be investigated with 57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy, which is a crucial instrument for identifying the crystallographic sites that are deficient in iron. The room temperature 57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy (RT-MS) of SLFCO is presented in Fig. 6 (a). M−type HFs contain 24 Fe3+ ions spread across 5 unique sites. 12 k (12 Fe3+ ions), 4f2 (4 Fe3+ ions), and 2a (2 Fe3+ ions) octahedral sites, 4f1 (4 Fe3+ ions) tetrahedral site, and 2b (2 Fe3+ ions) bipyramidal site. According to the Gorter model, there are eight antiparallel spins (12 k, 2b, and 2a) and four parallel spins (4f1 and 4f2) in a ferrimagnetic structure that are related by super-exchange interactions with the O2– ions [51] (see Fig. 6(b)).

The fitted RT-MS has five sextets that coincide with the previous five distinct crystallographic sites: 2b, 2a, 4f2, 4f1, and 12 k. The normal distribution of the 24 Fe3+ ions through these crystallographic sites is in proportions of 8:8:17: 17:50. The hyperfine parameters; isomer shift δIS, quadrupole splitting (Q.S.), hyperfine field (Hhf)., and area ratios (AO) are listed in Table 3 .

The hyperfine fields follow the sequence Hhf (4f2) > Hhf (2a) > Hhf (4f1) > Hhf (12 k) > Hhf (2b) in accordance with the literature [52,53]. The Hhf value of a magnetic ion's nucleus fluctuates in response to variations in its magnetic moment and environment.

In this study, with La3+-Cu2+ substitution, the Hhf value of 12 k and 2a sites increased while that of 2b, 4f1, and 4f2 decreased. The Fe3+–O2– distance parallel to the c-axis increases, which will cause the Fe3+-O-Fe3+ super-exchange interaction strength to decrease with the lattice expansion in SLFCO HF. This observation is also in accordance with the Mössbauer study of Sr0.7RE0.3Fe12-2x CoxAlxO19 [54]. The increase in the c-axis decreases the bond angle of (2b)–O2–-(4f2) and then reduces the Hhf contribution, which is transferred from the Fe-spins at the 4f2 sites onto the 57Fe nuclei at the 2b site.

Due to charge conversion, there may be a correlation between the increased presence of Fe2+ and the increase in δIS value of the 2a site with substitution [52]. The rise in the δIS value at the 12 k site may be attributed to the substitution of Cu2+ at this particular site. Four 12 k sites surround the 12 k site Fe3+ at the shortest site-site distance in the unit cell. Since Cu2+ has a lower electronegativity (1.517) than Fe3+ (1.687), hence in Fe3+(Cu2+)–O2-Fe3+, the valence electrons tend to spend more time in the vicinity of Fe3+, which ultimately increases the s-electrons screening at Fe3+ electron. Similarly, the δIS value for the 4f2 site will increase too since it is surrounded by two 12 k sites [52].

The combination of chemical and lattice expansion effects resulting from the La3+-Cu2+ substitution is responsible for the observed increase in the δIS value of the 2b site. The 2b sites are parallel to the c-axis and situated in the same plane as Sr2+(La3+). The electron density at the 2b Fe3+ nucleus will drop as the c-axis elongates, increasing the δIS value [55,56].

Based on the theoretical values of the normal distribution and literature [52,55,57,58], and from Fig. 6(a) and Tables 3, Clearly, the 12 k and 4f2 site area decline by 5.51 % and 3.86 % with La3+-Cu2+ substitution in Sr-hexaferrite, respectively. This provides clear evidence that out of all the octahedral sites, Cu2+ ions are more likely to occupy the 12 k and 4f2 sites.

Asymmetry in the distribution of charge and local crystal lattice distortion is measured by quadrupole splitting (Q.S.). The Q.S. value for every sextet falls within the range of 0 and 1 except for the 2b site. The high values of Q.S. at the 2b site are attributed to the asymmetry in charge distribution in trigonal bipyramidal geometry [59].

The coating effect on the various magnetic parameters and RT hysteresis curve is studied using VSM, as illustrated in Fig. 6(c). The obtained magnetic parameters, i.e., coercivity (Hc), saturation magnetization (Ms), remnant magnetization (Mr), and squareness ratio (Mr/Ms) are tabulated in Table 4 . A hard ferromagnetic behavior for both samples is confirmed based on the resulting hysteresis curve. The ferromagnetic phase is confirmed by Arrott–Belov–Kouvel (ABK) plots, which show a convex curve indicating spontaneous magnetization values even at zero applied field [60]. The ABK plot is represented in Fig. 6(d), while the spontaneous magnetization (SM) at zero field is displayed in Table 4.

The variation in magnetic parameters can be explained by the distribution of cations on various sub-lattice sites. The low magnetization of the parent SLFCO sample can be attributed to the preferential substitution of Cu ions at the spin-up 12 k and spin-down 4f2 sites (concluded from Mössbauer analysis) leading to the weakening of the superexchange interactions between spin-down and spin-up sublattices. This results in spin canting as mentioned in the previous work [13,14].

As shown in Table 4, the value of Hc increases after coating. This increase in coercivity can be attributed to the size shrinkage and intrinsic defects indicated by the XRD results. Furthermore, coating SLFCO NPs with CQDs affects the system’s homogeneity including cracks, roughness, porosity, etc. This alteration affects the surface magnetic anisotropy and consequently, the Hc of the sample [61].

On the other hand, the Ms and Mr values for the SLFCO/CQDs showed lower values for both parameters compared to the parent sample (SLFCO). These lower values may be attributed to the presence of non-magnetic OR-CQDs.

Additionally, the strain between the SLFCO NPs and the surface of CQDs creates surficial distortion as well as defects in crystallites, causing spin disorder at the interface of the two phases owing to the increased surface-to-volume ratio. These factors are the main issues in reducing the Ms and Mr values.

The law of approach to saturation (LAS) can be utilized to determine the value of Ms using the following equation.
(9)

Where: M and H stand for magnetization and applied field, respectively. In addition, χ represents high field susceptibility. α and β denote the inhomogeneity parameter and the anisotropy parameter, respectively.

According to Neel and Brown, in a highly applied field, the value of the two terms α/H and χH in equation (9) could be neglected. So, the relation between M and H may finally be formulated as:

The resulting fitted data is presented in Fig. 7 . Each sample produces a straight line, suggesting that the
α / H
and χH terms have a negligible influence. The intercept of the straight lines was used to determine the saturation magnetization [62].

The optical properties of SLFCO and SLFCO/CQDs were studied via UV–Vis-NIR absorbance and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). As seen in Fig. 8 (a), the RT absorbance spectra in the range of 200 to 2000 nm were recorded. SLFCO shows an absorption peak at 520 nm while SLFCO/CQDs exhibit an absorption peak centered at 567 nm. The spectra's redshift indicates a decrease in the energy gap (Eg). It is also, evident that loading the CQDs for M−type HFs increases the absorption intensity; indicating that the CQDs' broad absorption ability enhances the photon absorptive capacity for SLFCO/CQDs. Additionally, the diffuse reflectance (DRS) spectra for both samples are included as an inset in the absorption graph.

The energy gap value is estimated from the linear extrapolation of the plot between [F(R)hν]2 versus the energy (hν) in eV (Fig. 8(b)) based on equation (11).
(11)
F
R
h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
β
Where h is the plank’s constant, ν is the frequency of vibration, and A is a constant.

The exponent
β
value correlates with the transition type; whether it is allowed and direct or not [63]. In this case,
β
is equal to 1/2 as the transition is direct and allowed. The Eg value is found to be 1.68 eV and 1.76 eV for SLFCO and SLFCO/CQDs, respectively. The reduction in Eg suggests that the addition of CQDs to HFs modifies their energy band structure, facilitating the more efficient use of visible light [64] Sunlight can be utilized as a light source since it encompasses 45 % of the visible spectrum.

To gain insight into the localized states in the band gap caused by potential defects or vacancies in the sample, Urbach energy (Eu) is an important optical parameter. According to Eq. (12), the Eu value can be calculated from the reciprocal of the straight-line slope of the graph between ln (F(R)) versus hν as shown in Fig. 8(c).
(12)

)
The Urbach energy values are found to be 0.50 eV and 0.53 eV for SLFCO and SLFCO/CQDs, respectively. The addition of OR-CQDs with SLFCO changes the width of the localized states in the optical band gap. Thus, there is more system disorder and a greater density of localized states in the core–shell system, which is reflected in the higher EU value, a reduction in the optical band gap energy, dislocation density, and distortion parameter as mentioned previously.

It is commonly known that a material's ability to absorb electromagnetic radiation depends on several variables, including the material’s surface morphology, density, refractive index (n), and extinction coefficient (k) [65].

The term “skin depth”, also known as penetration depth (
δ d
), refers to the depth at which radiation intensity at the surface drops to 1/e of its starting value. The
δ d
and absorption coefficient (α) may be connected using the formula δd = 1/α, where α is assumed to be equal to F(R)) [66]. Fig. 8(d) clearly shows a definite and substantial decrease in skin depth for both samples as photon energy increases.

The most significant optical property of an optical material is “n”, which can be determined using the following equations since it is directly linked to the electronic polarizability of ions and the local field inside materials [67].
(13)
n

]

The variation of n with λ is displayed in Fig. 8(e). Both samples show normal dispersion up to 600 nm, after which they exhibit anomalous dispersion with a maximum of 2.88 and 2.69 for SLFCO and SLFCO/CQDs, respectively. With the addition of OR-CQDs as a shell. For mostoptics’applications, both values are within the acceptable range [68,69].

In addition, the coefficient of extinction (k) represents the amount of light lost in the samples and measures how much light is absorbed or dispersed per unit volume. It is the imaginary part of the complex refractive index. The value of k was determined for the samples using the following equation
(14)
k
λ
= α λ / 4 π
As seen in Fig. 8(f), the values of k increase as λ increases especially in the range of 480–580 nm. This increase is explained by a significant increase in absorption in this region, leading to an increase in energy loss [70]. In the wavelength range of 350 – 605 nm both α and k values show a rapid decrease. This decrease can be attributed to the core–shell system scattering more photons resulting in a decrease in energy reduction ability.

The PL spectra for the CQDs may exist independently of the carbogenic core or can be attributed to it resulting in multiple emitting sites. In this case, neither the CQDs core nor the surface functional groups fully govern the emission characteristics.

First, the QDs are excited by absorbing energy, usually in the form of photons. This energy is used to create electron-hole pairs. The core–shell configuration establishes a physical barrier that restricts the movement of excited electrons or holes within the shell. Additionally, the magnetic nature of the hexaferrite core can affect the electronic properties of the shell through magnetic interactions.

Secondly, the recombination between electron and hole can occur radiatively, where the energy is released as a photon, or non-radiatively, where the energy is dissipated as heat. Radiative recombination is the process that leads to the emission of light from the QDs [71].

The energy of the emitted photons can be adjusted by altering the size, composition, and structure of the CQDs [72]. Additionally, the SLFCO core can impact the emission characteristics by modifying the strain, electronic structure, and defect density within the shell.

The shell serves a dual purpose by not only confining the charge carriers but also passivating the core's surface, which diminishes non-radiative recombination pathways and enhances the quantum yield. Additionally, the shell provides protection to the SLFCO core against environmental degradation, thereby improving the stability and overall performance. On the other hand, the SLFCO core, being magnetic, can interact with external magnetic fields, which can be used to control the emission properties. This is particularly interesting for applications in magneto-optical devices.

For OR-CQDs, it is found that the maximum emitting peaks are excited by the light in the region of the π − π * transition. Meanwhile, the tunable multicolor emission phenomenon (Fig. 9 (a)) can be attributed to a general graphitic carbon core property [73], as well as the quantum confinement effect or energy traps of surface functional groups [72], and various surface defects [74]. Herein, the fluorescence of CQDs originates from surface-state emission [75]. More surface traps are present in the OR-CQDs sample, as a result, the energy levels are constrained.

Fig. 9(b) shows the RT photoluminescence (PL) spectra for SLFCO and SLFCO/CQDs. The PL spectra for both samples show emission peaks in the visible range. The excitation wavelength of 490 nm was selected to study the samples' emission and resulted in intense yellow-green emission (an intense peak is obtained at 545 nm). PL might occur due to the recombination of electrons and holes [76]. Small shifts in PL emissions of SLFCO compared to those of the SLFCO/CQDs core–shell were observed. Similar emission peaks were also reported for CuFe2O4/ZnFe2O4 core/shell nanostructure and PVC/MgFe2O4/multi-walled carbon nanotubes by other researchers [77,78]. The intensity of the SLFCO/CQDs emissions is smaller compared to the parent core. This is often attributed to the suppression of the recombination of electron-hole pairs by the addition of CQDs and considerable changes in particle size. This is also consistent with the increased surface defects that act as traps for carriers and reduce photo-generated electron-hole recombination. The broad emission peak is typical of the parent and core–shell samples, but, no characteristic peaks of the CQDs were observed.

Additionally, a shoulder for the oxygen vacancies in the active phase at about 600–650 nm was detected [79,80]. A deep-level defect known as ‘lattice disorder’ is indicated by the peaks around 700–720 nm [81,82].

Regarding the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism, it is likely attributed to size confinement. This confinement can be elucidated by the reduction in the bond length of the superexchange interaction within the nano-crystalline core–shell sample, consequently altering the electronic structure. The observed photoluminescence reveals intrinsic and extrinsic bands falling within the visible spectrum [83].

Moreover, color coordinates obtained from the CIE 1931 chromaticity map have been used to characterize the characteristic emissions (Fig. 9(c)). At 490 nm excitation wavelength, SLFCO/CQDs and SLFCO were found to have color coordinates of (0.415, 0.577) and (0.415, 0.576), respectively, attributed to yellow-green emission.

The estimated quantum yield of OR-CQDs and SLFCO/CQDs is about 88 % and 53 %, respectively under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 9(d-f)). Interestingly, the obtained values of QY are higher compared to the reported one for carbon dots @ ferrite by F. Mohandes et al. [17]. Accordingly, high-QY material commonly has a diversity of applications in the optical, biological, and energy areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of SLFCO/CQDs core–shell in ion metal sensing
Selectivity of SLFCO/CQDs core–shell with different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the complexity of the factors involved, the metal ion sensing process is still a topic of discussion. Ion bonding has been suggested by researchers as one of the mechanisms. Colorimetric and fluorescence techniques can be used to identify the interaction between the sample and metal ions. Typically, the colorimetric approach relies on the human eye [84].

Using the fluorescence approach, changes in the emission spectra resulting from chemical processes, such as quenching, are measured and monitored using fluorescence spectroscopy. Quenching can occur through various processes, including non-radiative recombination, energy transfer to quenchers, charge transfer processes, surface states and defects, and concentration quenching [85,86].

The fluorescence quenching effect (FQE) of various metal ions, such as Cr3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and K+, was used to test the selectivity of the prepared core–shell. The fluorescence intensity was measured; Io denotes the blank fluorescence intensity, and I indicate the fluorescence intensity following the addition of each metal ion of concentration and excitation wavelength of 285 nm (Fig. 10 (a)). Regarding Zn2+ and Cd2+ ions, the SLFCO/CQDs core–shell NPs exhibited good sensitivity, while Cr3+ and Ni2+ ions barely had an effect on the sample's fluorescence. Interestingly, K+ is found to have a strong FQE.

As reported in our previous work [75] and confirmed by FT-IR results, the SLFCO/CQDs core–shell NPs contain several surface functional groups such as hydroxyl (C-OH), carboxyl groups (COOH), carbonyl groups (C=O, C–H), and epoxide groups (C–O–C). These groups may help bind heavy metal ions to the surface of the sample, changing the surface state and having a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity as seen in Fig. 10(b) [24]. Furthermore, the –OH and COO-terminated groups on the surface of the sample may form hydrogen bonding leading to the formation of a cyclic structure [25]. The intriguing aspect of this cyclic structure around the SLFCO/CQDs is the presence of a cavity.

According to the size of the SLFCO/CQDs sample, these cavities are large enough to accommodate the selectivity of K+ ions. After the inclusion of K+ into these cavities, strong interactions between the K+ and the lone pairs of the oxygen donor atoms might lead to a more stable structure, providing a rationale for the selective stability imparted by K+ binding to these structures, thus, resulting in the quench of fluorescent.
These findings suggest that the prepared core–shell NPs exhibit high sensitivity to K+ ions and could be utilized as fluorescence sensor probes for detecting K+ ions.

The synthesized SLFCO/CQDs sensor was investigated using different K+ ion strengths at RT and an excitation wavelength of 285 nm. A K+ ion concentration ranging from 10 ppm to 50 ppm showed a dependent reduction in the fluorescence characteristics due to the formation of a metal hydroxide complex on the surface of the prepared core–shell as observed in Fig. 10(c). The Stern-Volmer plot displays the calibration curve for the relationship between fluorescence intensity and K+ concentration, which is linear with a regression coefficient (R2) = 0.9 as shown in Fig. 10(d). The limit of detection, (LOD) isthe lowest concentration that can be detected statisticallyusingaspecificanalyticaltechnique. It is determined using the following equation (15)
(15)
L O D = 3 σ / S
Where S stands for the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot and
σ
represents the standard deviation of blank solutions. In the present study, the LOD was found to be 15 ppm. This indicates that the synthesized SLFCO/CQDs core–shell sensor is efficient in detecting K+ ions.
The SLFCO/CQDs core–shell has been successfully used as a sensor at different K+ ion strengths at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 285 nm.

This study introduces a novel core–shell structure, synthesized for the first time using hexaferrite NPs and green synthesized CQDs with a low cost, easy preparation, and environmentally friendly approach. The material has the potential to be used as a sensor for detecting a variety of metal ions, despite having lower sensitivity than alternative composites identified in the survey [87,88].

HR-TEM confirmed the formation of a hexaferrite/carbon quantum dots core–shell (SLFCO/CQDs) at the nano-scale. XRD analysis revealed a noticeable decrease in crystallite size which aligns with the particle size values observed in HR-TEM images. The reduction in magnetization from 56 emu/g to 47 emu/g can be attributed to the addition of a diamagnetic carbon shell. In addition, the optical characteristics provide an enhancement in absorption for the SLFCO/CQDs sample along with a reduction in the band gap energy (Eg) from 1.76 to 1.68 nm. The prepared SLFCO/CQDs material demonstrated the ability to detect potassium ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 15 ppm thanks to the incorporation of CQDs as a shell over La-Cu doped Sr-hexaferrite. This makes the SLFCO/CQDs sample suitable for use in metal ion detection sensors.
The authors declare that all the data supporting the findings of this study are included in the article.

Ebtesam E. Ateia: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Obaida Rabie: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Amira T. Mohamed: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
